title: A GENERALIZED PROBABILITY KERNEL ON DIS - CRETE DISTRIBUTIONS AND ITS APPLICATION IN TWO - SAMPLE TEST

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - A GENERALIZED PROBABILITY KERNEL ON DIS - CRETE DISTRIBUTIONS AND ITS APPLICATION IN TWO SAMPLE TEST

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

We propose a generalized probability kernel(GPK) on discrete distributions with finite support. This probability kernel, defined as kernel between distributions instead of samples, generalizes the existing discrepancy statistics such as maximum mean discrepancy(MMD) as well as probability product kernels, and extends to more general cases. For both existing and newly proposed statistics, we estimate them through empirical frequency and illustrate the strategy to analyze the resulting bias and convergence bounds. We further propose power-MMD, a natural extension of MMD in the framework of GPK, illustrating its usage for the task of two-sample test. Our work connects the fields of discrete distribution-property estimation and kernel-based hypothesis test, which might shed light on more new possibilities.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

We focus on the two-sample problem, which is given two i.i.d samples _{x_ 1 _, x_ 2 _, ...xn} , {y_ 1 _, y_ 2 _, ..., yn}_ , could we infer the discrepancy between underlying distributions they are drawn from. For such a problem, the option of hypothesis test(two-sample test) is most popular, and a variety of statistics in estimating the discrepancy is proposed. In recent years, RKHS based method such as maximum mean discrepancy(MMD) has gained a lot of attention. (Gretton et al., 2012) has shown that in a universal-RKHS _F_ , MMD( _F,_ _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) = 0 if and only if _**p**_ = _**q**_ , thus could be used for the two-sample hypothesis test. (Gretton et al., 2012) further provides unbiased estimator of MMD with fast asymptotic convergence rate, illustrating its advantages.

On the other hand, estimating distribution properties with plugin(empirical) estimators on discrete setting is an active research area in recent years, where people focus on problem settings with large support size but not so large sample size. The Bernstein polynomial technique is introduced to analyze the bias of the plugin estimators in (Yi & Alon, 2020), which provides remarkable progress on bias-reduction methods of the plugin estimators. It is thus interesting to ask if the plugin estimators could motivate new results for the RKHS-based two-sample test.

Another interesting topic is about the probability kernel, defined as kernel function over probabilities, instead of over samples. As is easily seen, any discrepancy measure of distribution _**p**_ and _**q**_ could potentially be valid probability kernels, not so much work focuses on this. While (Jebara et al., 2004) introduced the so called probability product kernels which generalize a variety of discrepancy measures, its properties remain further study.

Motivated by above observations, our work focuses on a specialized probability kernel function which is a direct generalization of sample-based RKHS methods such as MMD. We focus on using plugin-estimator as the default estimator of the kernel function we defined, and illustrate that with the help of Bernstein polynomial techniques, we could analyze the bias and convergence bounds of these plugin-estimators. Our work thus connects the fields of discrete distribution-property estimation and kernel-based hypothesis test, which brings interesting possibilities.

## 3 GENERALIZED PROBABILITY KERNEL

Probability kernel function, defined as kernel function between distributions instead of samples, is a natural extension of the idea of kernel function in sample space.

**Definition 1.** _Given distribution_ _**p** and_ _**q** belongs to a family of discrete distribution with the same finite support {v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, ..., vk}, vi ∈_ R _[d] , where k is the support size, we define the probability kernel function as_ PK( _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ) _, which is a kernel function maps from_ R _[k] ×_ R _[k] to real value_ R _._

Many discrepancy measures, such as MMD, can serve as probability kernel functions, but people usually don’t use the term of probability kernel function when describing them. The reason is that for most of the time, we only consider a limited number of distributions, and do not need or have the resources to navigate through all the distributions within the family. For example, when looking into the two-sample problem, we usually assume two samples _{x_ 1 _, x_ 2 _, ..., xn} ∈_ R _[d]_ and _{y_ 1 _, y_ 2 _, ..., yn} ∈_ R _[d]_ are i.i.d drawn from two distributions _**p**_ and _**q**_ , and use the discrepancy measure MMD[ _F,_ _**p** ,_ _**q**_ ] to determine if _**p**_ and _**q**_ are indistinguishable in the RKHS _F_ . We do not consider all other distributions in _F_ that is irrelevant to our samples! So far the idea of kernel function between distributions is in practice not so much useful, however, here in this paper, we propose, when considering the plugin-estimator of many of the existing discrepancy measures, it is beneficial to view them as probability kernel functions.

## 3.1.3 EXAMPLE 3: POWER-MMD AS MEMBERS OF POLYNOMIAL GPK

Another interesting subset of polynomial GPK is the one extends MMD case into a power form and we denote it as power-MMD:

Apparently, MMD is a special case of power-MMD where _ρ_ = 1, and power-MMD satisfies the requirement in Theorem 1, thus has the potential usage of discrepancy measure. In section 5., we will show that power-MMD has unbiased estimator with analyzable convergence bounds thus could be used for two-sample test.

## 3.2 DISCUSSION OF GPK IN DISCRETE SETTING

As one may easily notice, the definition of GPK includes a gram matrix generated by the kernel function RK( _vi, vj_ ) which measures the discrepancy between _vi, vj ∈{v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, .., vk}_ . While considering the cases of categorical distribution, values of discrete variables does not relate to any notion of distance, this raises the question: how the introduced gram matrix will be beneficial in any cases?

The answer is twofold: 1. Many natural processes produce discrete distributions where there possibly exists a similarity measure in values which imply the similarity in frequencies of occurrence(probability values). For example, in the field of natural language process(NLP), one may treat words as atomic units with no notion of similarity between words, as these are represented as indices in a vocabulary. However, given large number of training samples, similarity measure between words could be made possible using techniques such as words2vec(Mikolov et al., 2013). Such techniques generally result in better performance and have become the important preprocessing techniques for NLP tasks(Goodfellow et al., 2016). 2. As there are cases where the values of discrete variables are totally irrelevant, or people may use kernel function RK( _vi, vj_ ) which doesn’t correctly imply the similarity in probability values, the GPK framework may still capture the similarity between distributions. One example is the case of MMD, which is, as we discussed above, an element of GPK family. As proved in (Gretton et al., 2012), MMD is a distribution free measurement between two samples, which means no matter what kind of _**p** ,_ _**q**_ and kernel( _x, y_ ), we have, the MMD[2] _F_[(] _**[p]**[,]_ _**[ q]**_[)][ measure will be][ 0][ if and only if] _**[ p]**_[=] _**[q]**_[.][However, the bad choice of kernel function] does have a negative effect on convergence bounds of the empirical estimator proposed in (Gretton et al., 2012), and will influence the results of two-sample test. For this reason, we mainly focus on dataset with known relativity measures in our experiment section.

## 4 PLUGIN-ESTIMATOR FOR GPK

So far we have defined the GPK and discussed some subsets of GPK with potential usage of two-sample test. Next we discuss how to build an estimator, given a member of GPK. In this section, we propose the plugin-estimator, which based on the count of occurrence of each value _vi ∈{v_ 1 _, v_ 2 _, ..., vk}_ in samples _{x_ 1 _, x_ 2 _, ..., xn} ∈_ _**p**_ or _**q**_ . We illustrate that by doing so, the techniques of Bernstein polynomial in (Qian et al., 2011) could be used to help building unbiased estimators for any members of polynomial GPK. Furthermore, we provide analyzable convergence bounds of these estimators.

## 6 SUMMARY

To summarize, we introduce the framework of generalized probability kernel(GPK). While GPK represents a large family of probability kernels, we focus on polynomial GPK since all members of such subset of GPK have unbiased plugin-estimators. Remarkably, a natural extension of MMD from the viewpoint of polynomial GPK, which we call power-MMD, could be used for two-sample test. Theoretical study shows that for _ρ_ = 1 case, power-MMD outperforms linear time MMD proposed in Gretton et al. (2012), and the performance of _ρ >_ 1 case is left for future work. For members of GPK which do not belong to polynomial GPK, it is not easy to design an unbiased estimators. However, bais reduction techniqes proposed in (Yi et al., 2018) and (Yi & Alon, 2020) could be used, and we still have the chance to apply two-sample test with the resulting estimators. Such a possibility is also left for future work.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a novel framework—generalized probability kernels (GPK)—that unifies and extends existing kernel-based two-sample tests like MMD to discrete distributions, with a focus on polynomial and power-MMD variants. It makes a valuable conceptual contribution by connecting discrete distribution estimation techniques (e.g., Bernstein polynomials) with kernel methods. The theoretical development is ambitious and the link to unbiased estimation via plugin estimators is well-motivated by recent work in property estimation.

Overview issues:

- Incorrect deviation bound in Theorem 3 due to misuse of McDiarmid's inequality: The deviation bound in Theorem 3 claims exponential concentration with a denominator of 2 in the exponent, but this appears to stem from an incorrect application of McDiarmid's inequality. The bound presented lacks dependence on the number of samples beyond constants, and the expression for Z is defined after the inequality, suggesting a circular derivation. More critically, the sensitivity terms τ depend on the random counts N_i, which themselves are functions of the data, violating the requirement in McDiarmid’s inequality that the bounded differences be deterministic. This undermines the validity of Corollary 3.2 and the proposed test in Corollary 3.3, which rely on this bound for type-I error control. The proof in the appendix attempts to use data-dependent Lipschitz constants, but this is not justified under standard forms of McDiarmid’s inequality. A proper analysis would require either uniform bounds on the sensitivity or a different concentration inequality such as Bernstein’s.

- Unjustified claim of improved performance over MMD for ρ=1 case: In Section 5.3.1, the paper asserts that the power-MMD test with ρ=1 'certainly has better performance' than the linear-time MMD of Gretton et al. (2012) because it provides a 'tighter bound'. However, the deviation bound in Corollary 3.2 is identical to that of Gretton et al. when ρ=1, as explicitly noted in Remark 3.2. There is no empirical or theoretical evidence provided that the test statistic itself has higher power under alternatives. The claim of superior performance is therefore unsubstantiated and misleading. At best, the method recovers the known result; at worst, it overstates its contribution by suggesting strict improvement without justification.

- Lack of power analysis for ρ>1 and no guidance on ρ selection: The paper introduces power-MMD with parameter ρ as a key innovation but provides no analysis of its power under alternatives. Section 5.3.2 admits there is 'no clear answer' to whether increasing ρ improves test performance. Without studying how the test statistic behaves when p ≠ q, or how power scales with ρ, sample size, or distributional differences, the practical utility of the method remains speculative. The numerical stability discussion in Corollary 3.4 does not compensate for this gap. A meaningful contribution would require either theoretical power analysis or simulations comparing different ρ values under structured alternatives, neither of which is included.

- Missing discussion of kernel choice and its impact on discrete distributions: While the paper acknowledges in Section 3.2 that the choice of base kernel RK(v_i, v_j) affects performance, it does not analyze how this choice interacts with the discrete nature of the problem. For instance, using a Gaussian kernel on categorical labels with arbitrary indexing could yield meaningless similarities. The paper defaults to assuming 'known relativity measures' but offers no principled guidance on constructing or selecting kernels for discrete supports. This is a significant omission, especially given that the GPK framework's benefit over standard MMD hinges on this kernel. Without such discussion, the method's applicability to real discrete data (e.g., genomics, NLP) is questionable.

- Ambiguous or incomplete estimator definition and test procedure: The plugin-estimator in Definition 7 splits samples into four parts, but the paper does not specify how this split should be done in practice (e.g., randomly, sequentially) or how the choice affects variance. The test statistic in Corollary 3.3 depends on Z, which involves random quantities like Φ and τ that depend on the observed counts. This makes the threshold data-dependent and potentially invalid for type-I error control, as the deviation bound assumes fixed constants. The test procedure is therefore not fully specified: should Z be estimated from data? If so, the concentration inequality no longer applies as written. This ambiguity undermines the practical implementation of the proposed test.

- No worked example showing GPK computation: The paper defines the generalized probability kernel (GPK) and its polynomial subclass but does not compute it for any concrete, fully-specified distribution pair. Readers are left without intuition for how the kernel behaves in practice. For instance, the authors should compute the power-MMD for a simple case such as two Bernoulli distributions with different success probabilities, using a specific base kernel (e.g., linear or identity Gram matrix). This would clarify how the mapping $\bm{p}^\rho - \bm{q}^\rho$ interacts with the Gram matrix and whether the resulting statistic increases with distributional divergence. Without such an example, the framework remains abstract and difficult to interpret.

- Missing simulation demonstrating non-vacuousness of power-MMD: The paper claims power-MMD generalizes MMD and may offer improved performance, but it does not show a single simulation where the test statistic successfully distinguishes two distributions under finite samples. A minimal demonstration would involve generating samples from two distinct discrete distributions (e.g., Zipf or multinomial) over a small alphabet, computing the power-MMD statistic for $\rho=1$ and $\rho=2$, and showing that it concentrates away from zero under the alternative. This is standard for two-sample test papers and necessary to show the result is non-vacuous. Without it, the theoretical bounds lack empirical grounding and the method's utility is unverified.

- No comparison to MMD under finite samples: While the paper claims power-MMD 'outperforms' linear-time MMD for $\rho=1$, it never verifies this claim numerically. It should compare the empirical variance, type-I error, and power of the two statistics on simulated data using the same base kernel and sampling scheme. For example, under $\bm{p} = \bm{q}$, both methods should control type-I error at the nominal level; under $\bm{p} \neq \bm{q}$, power-MMD should achieve higher rejection rates. Since the paper asserts strict improvement, it must demonstrate this in a controlled setting. Failing to do so leaves the central claim unsupported beyond algebraic equivalence.

- No guidance on base kernel selection for discrete supports: The GPK framework depends critically on the choice of base kernel $\mathsf{RK}(v_i, v_j)$, yet the paper offers no practical guidance for selecting it in discrete settings. For categorical data without natural metrics (e.g., genotypes, words), the identity kernel $K_{ij} = \delta_{ij}$ is a natural default, but this is never discussed. The authors should compute power-MMD under both identity and RBF kernels (with arbitrary labelings) to show whether results are sensitive to kernel choice. They could also suggest domain-specific kernels, such as edit distance for strings or co-occurrence similarity for words. Without such discussion, users cannot implement the method on real discrete data.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Correct the deviation bound in Theorem 3 using a valid concentration inequality with deterministic sensitivity bounds, or justify the use of data-dependent constants rigorously.

- Remove or substantially revise the claim in Section 5.3.1 that the ρ=1 case outperforms standard MMD, providing either theoretical power comparison or empirical evidence.

- Add a power analysis for power-MMD under alternative hypotheses, either theoretically or via simulations, to justify the use of ρ > 1.

- Include a discussion on practical kernel design for discrete supports, with recommendations or examples for categorical or ordinal data.

- Clarify the test procedure: specify how sample splitting is done and whether Z is treated as random or bounded uniformly; if the latter, provide worst-case bounds.

comments.title: Incorrect deviation bound in Theorem 3 due to misuse of McDiarmid's inequality
comments.quote:
has the convergence bound:

$\forall a>0,\Pr(\lvert\mathrm{GPK}_{E}[\mathcal{F},\phi,X,Y]-\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{GPK}_{E}[\mathcal{F},\phi,X,Y]]\rvert\geq a)\leq 2e^{-\frac{2a^{2}}{2}}$

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

where

$Z=\left(\left(n_{1}\left(\tau^{(1)}_{n_{1},m_{1}}\right)^{2}+m_{1}\left(\tau^{(2)}_{n_{1},m_{1}}\right)^{2}\right)\Phi^{2}_{m_{2},n_{2}}+\left(m_{2}\left(\tau^{(1)}_{m_{2},n_{2}}\right)^{2}+n_{2}\left(\tau^{(2)}_{m_{2},n_{2}}\right)^{2}\right)\Phi^{2}_{n_{1},m_{1}}\right)K_{max}^{2}$
$\Phi_{n,m}=\sum_{i\in[k]
comments.explanation: The stated bound simplifies to $2e^{-a^2}$, which is independent of sample sizes and the sensitivity terms, contradicting the standard form of McDiarmid’s inequality. The quantity $Z$ is clearly intended to be the sum of squared sensitivities, which must appear in the denominator of the exponent. The current form renders the bound vacuous, as it does not reflect how concentration improves with larger samples. The correct form should be $\Pr(\lvert\mathrm{GPK}_{E} - \mathbb{E}[\mathrm{GPK}_{E}]\rvert \geq a) \leq 2\exp(-2a^2 / Z)$. Moreover, $Z$ depends on random variables $\Phi_{n,m}$ and $\tau_{n,m}$, which are functions of the observed counts, violating the requirement that McDiarmid’s inequality uses deterministic bounds on the differences. A valid analysis would require uniform deterministic bounds on these terms.

comments.title: Unjustified claim of improved performance over MMD for ρ=1 case
comments.quote: Theoretical study shows that for $\rho=1$ case, power-MMD outperforms linear time MMD proposed in *Gretton et al. (2012)*, and the performance of $\rho>1$ case is left for future work.
comments.explanation: The paper asserts that power-MMD 'outperforms' linear-time MMD for $\rho=1$, but provides no evidence for this claim. As explicitly noted in Remark 3.2, the deviation bound for power-MMD at $\rho=1$ is identical to that of Gretton et al. (2012), and the estimator is equivalent to $\mathrm{MMD}_l^2$. No analysis of variance, power under alternatives, or finite-sample behavior is presented to justify superior performance. The claim appears to rest on the incorrect assertion in Section 5.3.1 that the bound is 'tighter', which contradicts the paper’s own Remark 3.2. Without a demonstrated improvement in statistical efficiency or test power, the claim of outperformance is unsubstantiated and misleading.

comments.title: Data-dependent normalization invalidates type-I error control
comments.quote: A hypothesis test of level $\alpha$ for the null hypothesis $\bm{p}=\bm{q}$ has the acceptance region $\left|\frac{\mathrm{GPK}_{E}[\mathcal{F},\phi_{\rho},X,Y]}{\sqrt{Z}}\right|<\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}log(\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)^{-1})}$ Where $Z$ is defined in Corollary 3.1
comments.explanation: The normalization factor $Z$ depends on the observed counts $N_i, M_i$ through $\Phi_{n,m}$ and $\tau_{n,m}$, making it a random variable under the null. Concentration inequalities used to derive fixed thresholds assume a deterministic variance proxy. When $Z$ is data-dependent and correlated with the numerator, the distribution of the test statistic can deviate from the assumed sub-Gaussian tail, potentially inflating type-I error beyond $\alpha$. A valid test would require a deterministic upper bound $\bar{Z}$ such that $Z \leq \bar{Z}$ almost surely, and use $\sqrt{\bar{Z}}$ in the denominator. Without such a bound, the proposed test does not guarantee level-$\alpha$ control.

comments.title: Sensitivity analysis ignores dual count update from single sample change
comments.quote:
Assume we freeze all but one element in  $X_{n_1} \coloneqq \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_s, \dots, x_{n_1}\}$ , and only  $x_s$  is allowed to change its value.

obviously, no matter how this element change, it always lies in the finite set of support  $\{v_{1}, v_{2}, \dots, v_{k}\}$ , without loss of generality, we assume  $x_{s}$  changes its value from  $v_{i}$  to  $v_{ii}$ , thus the corresponding count of occurrence  $N_{i}$  changes to  $N_{i} - 1$  and  $N_{ii}$  changes to  $N_{ii} + 1$
comments.explanation: When a single sample changes from $v_i$ to $v_{ii}$, two counts are affected: $N_i$ decreases by 1 and $N_{ii}$ increases by 1. The estimator $\mathrm{GPK}_E$ contains terms involving both $f_\phi(S_{i,n_1}, \cdot)$ and $f_\phi(S_{ii,n_1}, \cdot)$, so the total change in the estimator is the sum of the changes from both terms. The current analysis only accounts for one, underestimating the sensitivity by a factor of up to 2. This leads to an incorrect sum of squared differences in McDiarmid’s inequality, potentially invalidating the rate in the concentration bound. The per-sample sensitivity must reflect the joint update to both counts.

comments.title: Kernel-induced coupling amplifies sensitivity beyond per-term bound
comments.quote:
$$
\mathrm {G P K} _ {E} [ \mathcal {F}, \phi , X, Y ] = \sum_ {i \in [ k ]} \sum_ {j \in [ k ]} f _ {\phi} \left(S _ {i, n _ {1}}, S _ {i, m _ {1}}\right) K _ {i j} f _ {\phi} \left(S _ {j, m _ {2}}, S _ {j, n _ {2}}\right)
$$
comments.explanation: The sensitivity analysis must consider how a change in one count affects the entire double sum. When $N_i^{(n_1)}$ changes, the term $f_\phi(S_{i,n_1}, \cdot)$ appears in all $k$ terms of the sum over $j$. The total contribution of index $i$ is $f_\phi(S_{i,n_1}, \cdot) \sum_j K_{ij} f_\phi(S_{j,m_2}, \cdot)$, so a change in $f_\phi$ by $\tau_{n_1}$ affects the sum by $\tau_{n_1}$ times the magnitude of this sum. The current analysis appears to treat the effect as isolated to a single $(i,j)$ pair, but the kernel coupling through $K_{ij}$ means the sensitivity is amplified by the interaction with all other components. The bound on the per-sample difference must account for this aggregation, not just the local change in $f_\phi$.

comments.title: Positive definiteness assumption missing for GPK to be a discrepancy measure
comments.quote: $K$ is a semipositive definite matrix, thus by definition of positive definite matrix, $\bm{v}K\bm{v}^{T}\geq 0$, where equality holds if and only if $\bm{v}=\bm{0}$, and since $\bm{v}=\phi\circ(\bm{p},\bm{q})$, this condition further means $\phi\circ(\bm{p},\bm{q})=0$, which holds if and only if $\bm{p}=\bm{q}$.
comments.explanation:
The justification incorrectly assumes that $ m{v}K m{v}^T = 0$ implies $ m{v} =  m{0}$, which requires $K$ to be positive definite, not merely positive semidefinite. If $K$ is singular (e.g., due to duplicate support points or a non-characteristic kernel), there exist nonzero $ m{v}$ such that $ m{v}K m{v}^T = 0$. Thus, even if $\phi\circ( m{p}, m{q}) 
eq 0$, the GPK could vanish. The conclusion that $\mathrm{GPK}_{\mathcal{F},\phi}( m{p}, m{q}) = 0$ iff $ m{p} =  m{q}$ fails without assuming $K$ is strictly positive definite or that $\phi\circ( m{p}, m{q}) 
otin \ker(K)$ when $ m{p} 
eq  m{q}$. Add the assumption that $\mathsf{RK}$ is characteristic or that the support points are distinct.

comments.title: Unjustified convexity inequality in deviation bound derivation
comments.quote:
$\leq\sum_{i\in[k]}\left(\frac{N_{i}^{(n)}}{n}\right)^{\rho}+\sum_{i\in[k]}\left(\frac{N_{i}^{(m)}}{m}\right)^{\rho}$
$\leq\left(\sum_{i\in[k]}\frac{N_{i}^{(n)}}{n}\right)^{\rho}+\left(\sum_{i\in[k]}\frac{N_{i}^{(m)}}{m}\right)^{\rho}=2$
comments.explanation: The step $\sum_i (N_i^{(n)}/n)^\rho \leq (\sum_i N_i^{(n)}/n)^\rho$ is incorrect for $\rho > 1$. Since $x \mapsto x^\rho$ is convex for $\rho > 1$, Jensen's inequality gives $\sum_i p_i^\rho \leq 1$ for a probability vector, but $\sum_i p_i^\rho \leq (\sum_i p_i)^\rho$ does not hold in general. In fact, for $\rho > 1$, $\sum_i p_i^\rho \leq 1$ with equality only when one $p_i = 1$. The authors incorrectly apply superadditivity. While the final bound $\Phi_{n,m} \leq 2$ is correct (since each $\sum_i |g_\rho(N_i^{(n)}, n)| \leq \sum_i (N_i^{(n)}/n)^\rho \leq 1$), the justification is flawed and should be corrected using $\sum_i (N_i^{(n)}/n)^\rho \leq \max_i (N_i^{(n)}/n)^{\rho-1} \leq 1$.

comments.title: Ambiguous symmetry claim in GPK definition
comments.quote: Obviously, under this definition, the GPK is a symmetric probability kernel function where $\mathrm{GPK}_{\mathcal{F},\phi}(\bm{p},\bm{q})=\mathrm{GPK}_{\mathcal{F},\phi}(\bm{q},\bm{p})$
comments.explanation: The symmetry claim is not generally valid. The expression $\sum_{i,j}\phi\circ(p_i,q_i)K_{ij}\phi\circ(q_j,p_j)$ becomes $\sum_{i,j}\phi\circ(q_i,p_i)K_{ij}\phi\circ(p_j,q_j)$ when $\bm{p}$ and $\bm{q}$ are swapped. For this to equal the original, we need $\sum_{i,j} a_i K_{ij} b_j = \sum_{i,j} b_i K_{ij} a_j$ for $a_i = \phi\circ(p_i,q_i)$, $b_j = \phi\circ(q_j,p_j)$. Since $K$ is symmetric, this holds only if the vectors $\phi\circ(\bm{p},\bm{q})$ and $\phi\circ(\bm{q},\bm{p})$ yield the same quadratic form, which is not guaranteed unless $\phi\circ(a,b) = \phi\circ(b,a)$. Without such a restriction on $\phi$, symmetry fails. For example, if $\phi\circ(a,b) = a$, then $\mathrm{GPK} = \bm{p}^T K \bm{q}$, which is not symmetric in general. Add the condition that $\phi\circ(a,b) = \phi\circ(b,a)$ for all $a,b$.

comments.title: Incorrect binomial coefficient definition leads to division by zero
comments.quote:
g_{j}(k,n):=\left\{\begin{array}[]{lcl}g_{j}(k,n)=\left(\begin{array}[]{c}k\\
j\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}[]{c}n\\
j\end{array}\right)^{-1},&\text{for }j\leq k\\
0,&\text{for }j>k\end{array}\right.
comments.explanation: The expression $\binom{k}{j}\binom{n}{j}^{-1}$ is undefined when $j > n$, since $\binom{n}{j} = 0$ and division by zero occurs. Although the case $j > k$ is handled, the definition does not exclude $j > n$, which is essential because $\binom{n}{j}^{-1}$ appears explicitly. For example, if $l = o > n_1$, then $g_l(N_i^{(n_1)}, n_1)$ involves $\binom{n_1}{l}^{-1} = 0^{-1}$, which is undefined. The correct form used in Qian et al. (2011) defines such terms only for $j \leq \min(k, n)$, setting them to zero otherwise. This ensures the estimator is well-defined for all input values.

comments.title: Unjustified claim of universal unbiasedness for polynomial GPK
comments.quote: One of our main contributions of this paper is the proposal that we can always find an unbiased plugin-estimator for any members in polynomial GPK family.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'always' finding an unbiased estimator is incorrect without constraints on sample size. From Qian et al. (2011), $\mathbb{E}[g_l(N_i^{(n_1)}, n_1)] = p_i^l$ holds only when $l \leq n_1$. If the polynomial degree $o > n_1$ or $o > m_1$, then terms like $g_l(N_i^{(n_1)}, n_1)$ for $l > n_1$ are either undefined or zero, but $\mathbb{E}[0] = 0 \neq p_i^l$, introducing bias. For instance, if $\phi(p,q) = p^5 q^5$ and $n_1 = m_1 = 3$, the estimator fails to capture $p_i^5 q_i^5$. Thus, unbiasedness requires $o \leq \min(n_1, m_1)$. The current statement omits this, overstating the result.

comments.title: Invalid expectation factorization in proof of Theorem 2
comments.explanation: The proof assumes $\mathbb{E}[A_i]\mathbb{E}[B_i]\mathbb{E}[C_j]\mathbb{E}[D_j] = \mathbb{E}[A_i B_i C_j D_j]$, which requires joint independence of the counts. However, $N_i^{(n_1)}$ and $N_i^{(m_1)}$ both pertain to the same component $p_i$ and $q_i$. If these are estimated from overlapping data (e.g., a single sample split), they are not independent. The identity holds only under joint independence. The proof does not specify that the samples used to estimate $p_i$ and $q_i$ are independent — a condition necessary for unbiasedness. Add an explicit assumption that the counts are derived from four mutually independent subsamples.

comments.title: Missing condition for MMD to be a valid divergence
comments.quote: As proved in *(Gretton et al., 2012)*, MMD is a distribution free measurement between two samples, which means no matter what kind of $\bm{p},\bm{q}$ and $\mathsf{kernel}(x,y)$, we have, the $\mathrm{MMD}_{{\mathcal{F}}}^{2}(\bm{p},\bm{q})$ measure will be $0$ if and only if $\bm{p}=\bm{q}$.
comments.explanation: This claim misrepresents the conditions under which MMD is a valid statistical divergence. Gretton et al. (2012) show that $\mathrm{MMD}^2(\bm{p},\bm{q}) = 0$ iff $\bm{p} = \bm{q}$ only when the kernel is characteristic (e.g., universal). For a trivial kernel $k(x,y) = 1$, $\mathrm{MMD} = 0$ even when $\bm{p} \neq \bm{q}$. The property depends critically on the kernel, not just the distributions. The phrase 'no matter what kind of kernel' invalidates the claim. Rewrite to state that the property holds when the kernel is characteristic.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of $f_\phi$ function in plugin-estimator
comments.quote:
We define the plugin-estimator of $\mathrm{GPK}_{\mathcal{F},\phi}(\bm{p},\bm{q})$ as

$\mathrm{GPK}_{E}[\mathcal{F},\phi,X,Y]=\sum_{i\in[k]}\sum_{j\in[k]}f_{\phi}\left(S_{i,n_{1}},S_{i,m_{1}}\right)K_{ij}f_{\phi}\left(S_{j,m_{2}},S_{j,n_{2}}\right)$

where $f_{\phi}$ is a function related to function $\phi$, and $K$ is the gram matrix brought by $\mathcal{F}$.
comments.explanation: The function $f_\phi$ is used without a formal definition, only described as 'related to $\phi$'. Since $\phi$ maps probability values to feature space and $f_\phi$ operates on empirical counts, the mapping must correct for bias due to nonlinear transformation. For example, $\mathbb{E}[(N_i^{(n_1)}/n_1)^2] \neq p_i^2$, so a bias-corrected version is needed for unbiased estimation of $p_i^2$. Without specifying how $f_\phi$ adjusts for such bias—e.g., via U-statistic constructions or polynomial expansions—the estimator cannot be shown to be unbiased. Add a formal definition of $f_\phi$ in terms of $\phi$ and the multinomial sampling model.

comments.title: Incorrect claim of equivalence to linear-time MMD
comments.quote: When $\rho=1$, the power-MMD return to the original MMD case. Remarkably, the default-plugin-estimator of this case is equivalent to the linear time statistic $\mathrm{MMD}_{t}^{2}$ proposed in *(Gretton et al., 2012)*: $\mathrm{GPK}_{E}[\mathcal{F},\phi_{l},X,Y]=\mathrm{MMD}_{t}^{2}[\mathcal{F},X,Y]$ For details of the derivation, see appendix
comments.explanation: The claim of equivalence is unjustified. The linear-time $\mathrm{MMD}_t^2$ in Gretton et al. (2012) is a streaming U-statistic computed from kernel evaluations between individual points. In contrast, the default-plugin-estimator operates on binned count data and constructs a histogram-based V-statistic. At $\rho=1$, it becomes a function of relative bin frequencies, not individual kernel evaluations. These differ fundamentally in structure, computational design, and dependence on discretization. Even under an identity kernel, they do not coincide without additional limiting assumptions. The equivalence claim overreaches without explicit asymptotic or structural conditions.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a Generalized Probability Kernel (GPK) framework for discrete distributions, allowing for the construction of unbiased estimators using Bernstein polynomial techniques. The authors define a subclass of polynomial GPKs and demonstrate that power-MMD is a member of this class, providing tighter deviation bounds than existing linear-time MMD estimators.

The paper provides a clean theoretical connection between discrete property estimation and kernel-based two-sample testing. The derivation of unbiased estimators for polynomial kernels is a strong contribution, though the work lacks empirical validation to demonstrate the practical utility of the proposed power-MMD statistic.

Overview issues:

- Lack of empirical validation: The paper presents a significant theoretical framework but fails to provide any numerical experiments or simulations. While the theoretical derivation for the unbiased estimator is sound, the practical performance of the power-MMD statistic—specifically its power and numerical stability in finite-sample settings—remains untested. A top-tier venue requires at least a synthetic data simulation comparing the power of the proposed test against standard MMD and other baselines to justify the theoretical claims.

- Numerical instability of high-order power-MMD: Section 5.3.2 acknowledges that the power-MMD statistic involves terms that scale with the power parameter rho, which can lead to numerical instability when support counts are small relative to the sample size. The proposed upper bound in Corollary 3.4 is a step toward addressing this, but it is unclear whether this bound is tight enough to be useful for hypothesis testing in practice. The authors should demonstrate whether the test remains valid and powerful for rho > 1 using a concrete example or a stability analysis.

- Ambiguity in the choice of kernel K: The GPK framework relies on a Gram matrix K derived from a kernel on the support points. However, the paper does not specify how to choose this kernel when the support has no natural geometry, as is often the case in categorical data. Section 3.2 mentions that the kernel choice affects convergence bounds, but there is no guidance on selecting an optimal or robust K for the discrete setting. Without a discussion on how to learn or select K, the applicability of the GPK framework remains limited to cases where a distance metric on the support is already provided.

- Incomplete proof details in the appendix: The proof for Theorem 3 (deviation bound) relies on McDiarmid's inequality, but the derivation of the constant Z is opaque. Specifically, the paper assumes that the plugin-estimator has bounded differences, but the dependence of the constants tau on the sample counts needs more rigorous justification. The appendix should explicitly show how the bound on the fluctuations of the plugin-estimator is derived, particularly how the interaction between the polynomial coefficients alpha and the Bernstein basis functions affects the final concentration bound.

- Simulation-based validation of power-MMD test power: The paper claims that power-MMD with rho=1 outperforms existing linear-time MMD, yet provides no empirical evidence to support this or to explore the behavior of rho > 1. To demonstrate the practical utility of the framework, the authors should conduct a simulation on a synthetic discrete distribution, such as a multinomial distribution with varying support size k and varying distance between p and q. Specifically, plot the empirical power of the test as a function of the sample size n for different values of rho, comparing the results against the standard linear-time MMD baseline. This simulation is necessary to verify if the theoretical 'tighter bound' for rho=1 translates into improved rejection rates in finite-sample settings.

- Worked example of kernel selection for non-geometric support: The GPK framework requires a Gram matrix K, but the paper offers no concrete guidance on how to construct K when the discrete support lacks a natural distance metric. To address this, the paper should provide a worked example using a standard dataset, such as a small-vocabulary text corpus or a categorical classification task. The authors should demonstrate how to derive K using a specific embedding method, such as word2vec or a simple co-occurrence-based similarity, and show how this choice of K impacts the resulting test statistic. Providing this concrete implementation path would significantly clarify the practical applicability of the GPK framework.

- Empirical verification of numerical stability for rho > 1: Section 5.3.2 identifies potential numerical instabilities for power-MMD when rho > 1 and proposes an upper bound in Corollary 3.4 as a remedy. However, the paper does not evaluate whether this bound is actually tight enough to maintain the validity of the hypothesis test in practice. The authors should implement the proposed stable estimator and compare it against the direct plugin-estimator on a controlled experiment where the true distribution is known. This would allow the authors to quantify the trade-off between the increased sensitivity of higher-order power-MMD and the loss of numerical precision.

Recommendation: major revision. The theoretical framework is well-motivated and potentially useful, but the paper is incomplete without empirical evidence showing that the proposed estimators behave as expected. A revision must include a simulation study to validate the power and stability of the power-MMD test.

Revision targets:

- Add a simulation study comparing the power of power-MMD (for rho=1, 2, 3) against standard MMD on synthetic discrete distributions.

- Perform a numerical stability analysis for high-order power-MMD to demonstrate the effectiveness of the bound proposed in Corollary 3.4.

- Clarify the selection criteria for the Gram matrix K in the absence of a natural geometry on the support.

- Expand the appendix to provide a step-by-step derivation for the constants in Theorem 3.

For a non-characteristic kernel, such as a constant kernel, the MMD is identically zero for all distributions. Please clarify that this property holds specifically for characteristic kernels.

comments.title: Ambiguity in kernel function notation

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces a Generalized Probability Kernel (GPK) framework for discrete distributions and proposes power-MMD as a two-sample test statistic. While the theoretical setup connects polynomial kernels to plugin estimators, the manuscript contains several mathematical gaps, unverified claims, and a complete lack of empirical validation.

The attempt to unify MMD and probability product kernels under a polynomial GPK framework is conceptually interesting, and the use of Bernstein polynomial techniques for unbiased estimation shows familiarity with recent property estimation literature. The main weakness lies in the theoretical derivations, which contain logical gaps and overclaims, combined with an absence of experiments that would demonstrate whether the proposed test actually improves upon established baselines.

Overview issues:

- Positive definiteness assumption in Theorem 1 is mathematically insufficient: Theorem 1 claims that GPK is a discrepancy probability kernel when the Gram matrix K is positive semi-definite. The proof relies on the equivalence vKv^T = 0 iff v = 0, which strictly requires K to be positive definite. When K is only semi-definite, the kernel can vanish for non-zero vectors, breaking the necessary and sufficient condition for p = q. This gap undermines the theoretical foundation of the entire framework, as many standard kernels on finite supports produce singular Gram matrices. The authors should either restrict K to be strictly positive definite in Definition 2 or reformulate Theorem 1 to explicitly account for the null space of K and define the discrepancy property on the appropriate quotient space.

- McDiarmid bound derivation contains logical inconsistencies and overclaims: Theorem 3 applies McDiarmid’s inequality to derive a concentration bound, but the Lipschitz constants tau^(1) and tau^(2) depend on the random counts N_i^(n) and N_i^(m). McDiarmid’s inequality requires deterministic bounds on the function’s sensitivity to single-sample changes, not data-dependent quantities. Furthermore, the exponent in the theorem statement contains a typographical error, listing 2 in the denominator instead of Z. Corollary 3.2 also claims the resulting bound is a tighter version of the linear-time MMD bound from Gretton et al. (2012), yet the algebra shows the exponents are identical when rho = 1. The derivation needs to be rewritten with deterministic sensitivity constants, and the comparison to existing bounds must be corrected or replaced with a rigorous asymptotic analysis showing genuine improvement.

- Unbiased estimator validity ignores sample size thresholds and zero-frequency collapse: Theorem 2 introduces an unbiased plugin estimator using coefficients g_j(k,n) = binom(k,j)/binom(n,j), which are only well-defined when n >= j. For power-MMD with degree rho, the estimator requires n >= rho. The paper never states this constraint or discusses the behavior when n < rho, where the estimator collapses to zero. In sparse discrete settings where the support size k is large relative to n, this condition is frequently violated, rendering the estimator useless. The authors should explicitly state the n >= rho requirement, analyze the bias-variance trade-off near this threshold, and provide a practical fallback or regularized estimator for undersampled regimes.

- Complete absence of empirical validation for the proposed two-sample test: The paper proposes a two-sample test framework and derives theoretical bounds, yet provides zero experiments, simulations, or real-data benchmarks. Section 5.3 outlines a hypothesis testing procedure and Corollary 3.3 gives an acceptance region, but no type-I error rates or power curves are reported. The conclusion admits that performance for rho > 1 is left for future work, and even the rho = 1 case lacks empirical comparison against Gretton’s linear-time MMD. Without synthetic or real-world evaluations demonstrating competitive or superior power, the theoretical claims remain unverified. The revision must include a comprehensive experimental section with controlled distributional shifts, sample size sweeps, and baseline comparisons.

- Numerical stability analysis for rho > 1 is ad-hoc and lacks theoretical grounding: Section 5.3.2 correctly identifies that g_rho(N_i, n) decays exponentially as rho increases, causing numerical underflow when N_i << n. The proposed fix in Corollary 3.4 replaces sample sizes with S_N = max N_i to stabilize the ratio, but this modification alters the statistical properties of the estimator without justification. The resulting statistic no longer matches the unbiased estimator from Theorem 2, and its convergence behavior is unanalyzed. The authors should either derive a theoretically sound stabilized estimator, provide a rigorous error bound for the Corollary 3.4 approximation, or restrict the method to regimes where n comfortably exceeds rho.

- Overstated novelty relative to established discrete kernel methods: The paper positions GPK as a novel generalization of MMD and probability product kernels, but polynomial kernels on discrete distributions are already well-understood in the literature. The linear-time estimator derived in Section 4 is mathematically equivalent to Gretton et al. (2012)’s linear-time MMD, and the Gram matrix formulation does not introduce new computational or statistical advantages for categorical data. The discussion in Section 3.2 acknowledges that word embeddings or similarity measures are needed for K, which shifts the burden of kernel design to preprocessing rather than the proposed framework. The introduction should temper claims of generalization and explicitly clarify what theoretical or practical gaps GPK addresses beyond reparameterizing existing discrepancy measures.

- Concrete parametric example for power-MMD behavior: The paper develops the GPK framework entirely abstractly, leaving readers without a concrete model to calibrate their understanding. Computing power-MMD for a simple parametric family, such as a Bernoulli or multinomial distribution with a linear Gram matrix, would demonstrate how the statistic behaves under known distributional shifts. This computation would also illustrate how the polynomial degree $ho$ modulates sensitivity to frequency changes, which is central to the paper's claims about power-MMD's utility. Without a worked example, the theoretical definitions remain disconnected from practical intuition. Readers need to see the mechanics in action before trusting the general proofs.

- Practical estimation of deviation bound parameters: Corollary 3.3 derives a testing threshold that depends on the random variable $Z$ and constants like $	au$ and $	au$, which themselves rely on unknown sample counts and distribution properties. The paper does not explain how practitioners should estimate or bound these quantities during actual hypothesis testing. A discussion of feasible approximation strategies, or at least an acknowledgment of the computational burden when the support size $k$ is large, would bridge the gap between the theoretical bound and actual implementation. Readers will wonder how to apply the proposed test without a clear recipe for handling these intermediate terms. Providing a step-by-step estimation protocol is necessary for the method to be usable.

- Development of the claimed connection to property estimation: The introduction and conclusion repeatedly claim that the work connects discrete distribution-property estimation with kernel-based hypothesis testing, yet this link is never substantiated. The paper borrows Bernstein polynomial techniques to prove unbiasedness but stops short of analyzing how this framework improves upon or differs from direct property estimation methods for two-sample testing. Developing this implication would require a discussion on whether the GPK framework offers statistical or computational advantages over standard plugin estimators, or if it merely reparameterizes existing approaches. Filling this gap would justify the paper's central motivation. The promised synthesis between the two fields remains superficial without deeper analysis.

- Formal comparison to established discrete kernels: The paper claims power-MMD generalizes existing discrepancy measures but never verifies this claim against other standard kernels designed for discrete data, such as the chi-squared kernel or Fisher-Yates kernel. A formal comparison showing how GPK recovers these methods as special cases, or demonstrates superior handling of zero-frequency counts, would ground the proposed framework in the existing literature. Without this positioning, the claim of generalization remains unverified and the practical relevance of the polynomial GPK subset is unclear. The authors should explicitly map the GPK formulation onto these benchmarks to validate their theoretical assertions.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Correct the positive definiteness requirement in Theorem 1 and reformulate the discrepancy condition to handle semi-definite Gram matrices or restrict K to be strictly positive definite.

- Rewrite the McDiarmid concentration bound derivation with deterministic Lipschitz constants, fix the typographical error in Theorem 3, and correct the false claim of a tighter bound in Corollary 3.2.

- Explicitly state the n >= rho sample size constraint for the unbiased estimator, analyze the estimator's behavior near this threshold, and provide a practical fallback for undersampled regimes.

- Add a full experimental section with synthetic and real-world two-sample benchmarks, reporting type-I error control, power curves across sample sizes, and comparisons against linear-time MMD and other discrete kernel baselines.

comments.title: Abstract mischaracterizes estimator bias
comments.quote: This probability kernel, defined as kernel between distributions instead of samples, generalizes the existing discrepancy statistics such as maximum mean discrepancy(MMD) as well as probability product kernels, and extends to more general cases. For both existing and newly proposed statistics, we estimate them through empirical frequency and illustrate the strategy to analyze the resulting bias and convergence bounds.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims the paper analyzes the 'resulting bias' of the empirical frequency estimators. This directly contradicts the paper's core theoretical contribution, which establishes an unbiased plugin estimator. Unbiased estimators, by definition, have zero expectation error, so there is no bias to analyze. The authors likely meant to discuss variance or mean squared error. Rewrite 'resulting bias' as 'estimation variance' to accurately reflect the statistical properties proven in the manuscript.

comments.title: PSD/PD conflation breaks zero-equivalence proof
comments.quote: $K$ is a semipositive definite matrix, thus by definition of positive definite matrix, $\bm{v}K\bm{v}^{T}\geq 0$, where equality holds if and only if $\bm{v}=\bm{0}$, and since $\bm{v}=\phi\circ(\bm{p},\bm{q})$, this condition further means $\phi\circ(\bm{p},\bm{q})=0$, which holds if and only if $\bm{p}=\bm{q}$.
comments.explanation: The proof incorrectly assumes that a positive semi-definite (PSD) matrix satisfies $\bm{v}^T K \bm{v} = 0 \iff \bm{v} = \bm{0}$. This equivalence strictly requires positive definiteness (PD). When $K$ is only PSD, the quadratic form can vanish for non-zero vectors in the null space, breaking the necessary and sufficient condition for $p = q$. The authors must either restrict $K$ to be strictly positive definite or explicitly account for the null space of $K$ in the discrepancy definition.

comments.title: Ambiguous vector polynomial notation and coefficient typo
comments.quote: $\phi\circ(\bm{p},\bm{q})=\sum_{l=0}^{o}\sum_{s=0}^{o}\alpha_{l,s}\bm{p}^{l}\bm{q}^{s}$ where $o\in\mathbb{Z}$ is the degree of the polynomial, and $a_{l,s}\in\mathbb{R}$ is the coefficient
comments.explanation: The expression $\bm{p}^l \bm{q}^s$ is mathematically ambiguous when $\bm{p}$ and $\bm{q}$ are vectors in $\mathbb{R}^k$. It should explicitly specify that powers and products are computed element-wise (Hadamard product) to yield a vector output. Additionally, the text switches from $\alpha_{l,s}$ in the formula to $a_{l,s}$ in the description. Clarifying the element-wise operation and standardizing the coefficient symbol will prevent misinterpretation of the polynomial structure.

comments.title: Antisymmetric mapping yields non-positive discrepancy values
comments.quote: 1. symmetric or antisymmetric with respect to $\bm{p}$ and $\bm{q}$: $\phi\circ(\bm{p},\bm{q})=\phi\circ(\bm{q},\bm{p})$ or $\phi\circ(\bm{p},\bm{q})=-\phi\circ(\bm{q},\bm{p})$
comments.explanation: If $\phi$ is antisymmetric, then $\phi(\bm{q},\bm{p}) = -\phi(\bm{p},\bm{q})$, which causes the GPK to evaluate to $-\bm{v}K\bm{v}^T$. Since $K$ is positive semi-definite, $\bm{v}K\bm{v}^T \ge 0$, making the GPK non-positive. This contradicts the standard interpretation of a discrepancy measure or probability kernel, which are inherently non-negative quantities. The theorem should either restrict $\phi$ to be symmetric, or explicitly state that the absolute value or squared magnitude is taken to ensure non-negativity.

comments.title: Typographical negative sign in MMD-GPK equality
comments.quote: $=-\mathrm{GPK}_{\mathcal{F},\phi_{l}}(\bm{p},\bm{q})$
comments.explanation: The squared MMD is defined as a squared norm in an RKHS, guaranteeing non-negativity. The preceding algebraic expansion correctly yields the quadratic form $\sum_{i,j}(p_i-q_i)K_{ij}(p_j-q_j)$, which is non-negative for any positive semi-definite kernel matrix $K$. Equating this positive quantity to $-\mathrm{GPK}_{\mathcal{F},\phi_{l}}(\bm{p},\bm{q})$ implies the GPK is negative, contradicting its role as a discrepancy measure. This is a typographical error. Rewrite the final equality as $=\mathrm{GPK}_{\mathcal{F},\phi_{l}}(\bm{p},\bm{q})$ to preserve the required non-negativity.

comments.title: Asymmetric coefficient and mapping function notation
comments.quote: The product GPK is a subset of polynomial GPK where $\alpha_{l,0}=1$, and all other coefficients are $0$. the corresponding mapping function is: $\phi(\bm{p},\bm{q})=\bm{p}^{l}$
comments.explanation: The coefficient choice $\alpha_{l,0}=1$ yields a kernel term proportional to $p^l q^0 = p^l$, which breaks the required symmetry $K(\bm{p},\bm{q}) = K(\bm{q},\bm{p})$ for a valid kernel. To recover the symmetric probability product kernel $\bm{p}^l \bm{q}^l$ cited from Jebara et al. (2004), the polynomial coefficient must be $\alpha_{l,l}=1$. Furthermore, the mapping function is written as $\phi(\bm{p},\bm{q})=\bm{p}^{l}$, which incorrectly takes two arguments but only depends on one. The notation should be corrected to $\phi(\bm{p})=\bm{p}^{l}$ so the kernel evaluates to $\phi(\bm{p})^T K \phi(\bm{q})$.

comments.title: Restriction of l to integers limits kernel applicability
comments.quote: Let $\bm{p}$ and $\bm{q}$ be probability distributions on support $\{v_{1},v_{2},...,v_{k}\}$, and $l\in\mathbb{Z}$ be nonnegative integer. The product GPK is a subset of polynomial GPK where $\alpha_{l,0}=1$, and all other coefficients are $0$.
comments.explanation: The definition restricts $l$ to nonnegative integers ($l\in\mathbb{Z}$). However, standard probability product kernels (e.g., Bhattacharyya, Hellinger) commonly use fractional exponents like $l=1/2$ or arbitrary $l>0$. Restricting $l$ to integers unnecessarily limits the family of kernels and contradicts the broader literature on product kernels. The authors should either generalize $l$ to $l \in \mathbb{R}_{>0}$ or explicitly justify why integer powers are sufficient for their two-sample test application.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about MMD zero condition
comments.quote: As proved in *(Gretton et al., 2012)*, MMD is a distribution free measurement between two samples, which means no matter what kind of $\bm{p},\bm{q}$ and $\mathsf{kernel}(x,y)$, we have, the $\mathrm{MMD}_{{\mathcal{F}}}^{2}(\bm{p},\bm{q})$ measure will be $0$ if and only if $\bm{p}=\bm{q}$.
comments.explanation: The assertion that MMD equals zero if and only if $p=q$ 'no matter what kind of kernel(x,y)' is mathematically false. Gretton et al. (2012) explicitly establish that MMD is a metric on probability distributions only when the kernel is characteristic (or universal). With a non-characteristic kernel, MMD can vanish for distinct distributions, breaking the necessary and sufficient condition for two-sample testing. Rewrite the claim to specify that the zero-if-and-only-if property strictly depends on the characteristic property of the kernel.

comments.title: Implicit definition of f_phi leaves estimator undefined
comments.quote: where $f_{\phi}$ is a function related to function $\phi$, and $K$ is the gram matrix brought by $\mathcal{F}$.
comments.explanation: The estimator formula depends entirely on $f_\phi$, yet the text only states it is 'related to function $\phi$' without providing a mathematical definition or explicit mapping. Without specifying how $f_\phi$ transforms the sufficient statistics $(N_i, n)$, it is impossible to verify the claimed unbiasedness or analyze the convergence bounds. Add the explicit functional form of $f_\phi$ and demonstrate how it incorporates the Bernstein polynomial approximation to ensure the estimator targets the correct GPK quantity.

comments.title: Unclear relationship between total and split sample sizes
comments.quote: given two samples $\{x_{1},x_{2},...,x_{n}\}$, $\{y_{1},y_{2},...,y_{n}\}$ from $\bm{p}$ and $\bm{q}$, we depart each sample of $x$ and $y$ into two parts, yielding 4 different samples with size $n_{1}$, $n_{2}$, $m_{1}$, $m_{2}$
comments.explanation: The passage introduces both initial sample sizes as $n$, but subsequently splits them into $n_1, n_2$ and $m_1, m_2$ without stating how these quantities relate. This omission creates ambiguity regarding the empirical frequency calculations and the scaling of the estimator. Readers cannot determine if $n_1+n_2$ equals the original $n$, or if the two distributions originally had different sizes. Rewrite the sentence to explicitly state $n = n_1 + n_2$ and $m = m_1 + m_2$ (or clarify that the original sizes were $n$ and $m$ respectively) to establish consistent notation throughout the section.

comments.title: Unjustified cross-splitting structure in estimator formula
comments.quote: $\mathrm{GPK}_{E}[\mathcal{F},\phi,X,Y]=\sum_{i\in[k]}\sum_{j\in[k]}f_{\phi}\left(S_{i,n_{1}},S_{i,m_{1}}\right)K_{ij}f_{\phi}\left(S_{j,m_{2}},S_{j,n_{2}}\right)$
comments.explanation: The proposed estimator pairs $X_{n_1}$ with $Y_{m_1}$ in the first factor and $Y_{m_2}$ with $X_{n_2}$ in the second. While splitting samples is a standard technique for constructing unbiased quadratic estimators, this specific cross-pairing arrangement is not motivated or derived. Standard unbiased forms for cross-distribution functionals typically pair $X$ and $Y$ samples directly within each factor or use symmetric U-statistic constructions. Provide a brief derivation or reference showing why this particular cross-splitting yields an unbiased estimate of $\mathrm{GPK}_{\mathcal{F},\phi}(\bm{p},\bm{q})$.

comments.title: Notation mismatch between function arguments and internal variables
comments.explanation: The left-hand side declares the mapping function as taking $S_{i,n_1}$ and $S_{i,m_1}$ as inputs, yet the right-hand side explicitly substitutes the count variables $N_{i}^{(n_{1})}$ and $N_{i}^{(m_{1})}$. This inconsistency obscures the actual data flow into the estimator. It is unclear whether $S$ refers to the raw sample, the empirical frequency vector, or the category count itself. Rewrite the left-hand side as $f_{\phi}(N_{i}^{(n_{1}}),N_{i}^{(m_{1}}))$ to match the variables used in the summation, or explicitly define $S_{i,n}$ as the count variable earlier in the text.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated unbiasedness claim deferred to appendix
comments.quote:
$

###### Proof.

The basic idea is directly using the result of Bernstein polynomial in *(Qian et al., 2011)* to build unbiased estimators. We put our formal proof in appen
comments.explanation: The section asserts that the proposed mapping yields an unbiased estimator but provides no derivation, instead deferring the proof to the appendix and citing an external paper. While the reference is relevant, the logical bridge between the cited Bernstein polynomial results and the discrete multinomial expectation $\mathbb{E}[N_j/n^j] = p^j$ is missing. Readers cannot verify the unbiasedness claim without leaving the section. Add a brief derivation showing $\mathbb{E}[g_j(N_i^{(n)}, n)] = p_i^j$ using the binomial probability mass function, and explicitly state how this connects to the cited Bernstein polynomial framework.

comments.title: Ambiguity in polynomial mapping over probability vectors
comments.quote: polynomial mapping function $\phi(\bm{p},\bm{q})=\sum_{l=0}^{o}\sum_{s=0}^{o}\alpha_{l,s}\bm{p}^{l}\bm{q}^{s}$
comments.explanation: The notation $\bm{p}^{l}$ and $\bm{q}^{s}$ is ambiguous. If $\bm{p}$ and $\bm{q}$ are probability vectors, does the polynomial operate element-wise on each category $i$, or does it aggregate across categories? The proposed estimator $f_\phi$ only uses the count $N_i^{(n_1)}$ for a single category $i$, implying an element-wise interpretation. However, if $\phi$ is intended to capture cross-category interactions (e.g., $p_1 p_2$), the estimator is fundamentally incomplete because it ignores joint category counts. Rewrite the polynomial definition as $\phi(p_i, q_i) = \sum_{l=0}^{o}\sum_{s=0}^{o}\alpha_{l,s} p_i^l q_i^s$ to explicitly match the component-wise scope of the proposed estimator.

comments.title: Typographical error in concentration bound exponent
comments.quote: $\forall a>0,\Pr(\lvert\mathrm{GPK}_{E}[\mathcal{F},\phi,X,Y]-\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{GPK}_{E}[\mathcal{F},\phi,X,Y]]\rvert\geq a)\leq 2e^{-\frac{2a^{2}}{2}}$
comments.explanation: The denominator in the exponent is incorrectly typeset as 2. Given the explicit definition of $Z$ immediately following the inequality, the bound should read $2e^{-\frac{2a^{2}}{Z}}$. This typographical error renders the stated bound dimensionally inconsistent with the defined sensitivity parameters and contradicts the standard form of McDiarmid's inequality. Rewrite the inequality with $Z$ in the denominator to align with McDiarmid's inequality.

comments.title: Data-dependent sensitivity parameters violate McDiarmid's conditions
comments.quote: $\tau^{(1)}_{n,m}=\sup_{i\in[k]}\left(\sum_{l=0}^{o}\sum_{s=0}^{o}\frac{l}{N^{(n)}_{i}}\cdot|\alpha_{l,s}|\cdot g_{l}(N^{(n)}_{i},n)g_{s}(N^{(m)}_{i},m)\right)$
comments.explanation: McDiarmid's inequality requires deterministic bounds on the function's sensitivity to single-sample changes. Here, $\tau^{(1)}_{n,m}$ and $\tau^{(2)}_{n,m}$ depend on the random sample counts $N^{(n)}_i$ and $N^{(m)}_i$. Using data-dependent quantities in the concentration exponent violates the foundational assumptions of McDiarmid's inequality. The derivation must either replace these with their worst-case deterministic bounds (e.g., evaluating at the minimum possible count $N_i=1$) or provide a rigorous justification for applying the inequality with random sensitivity parameters.

comments.title: Undefined sample sizes in the variance proxy definition
comments.explanation: The expression for $Z$ introduces undefined sample sizes $n_2$ and $m_2$. The theorem statement and preceding context only define $n_1$ and $m_1$ as the sample sizes for the two distributions. The sudden appearance of $n_2$ and $m_2$ in the second term of $Z$ creates an ambiguity regarding which sample sizes are used for the cross-terms. Clarify the relationship between $n_1, m_1$ and $n_2, m_2$, or correct the indices to match the defined sample sizes consistently throughout the variance proxy.

comments.title: Undefined kernel matrix K in sensitivity bound
comments.quote: $K_{max}$ is the largest value of entries in $K$
comments.explanation: The symbol $K$ is introduced without definition in the theorem statement or the surrounding text. In the context of GPK (Generalized Probability Kernel), $K$ likely refers to a kernel matrix or a set of basis functions, but its precise definition and relationship to the plugin estimator are missing. Without defining $K$, the bound's constant $K_{max}$ is meaningless. Add a definition for $K$ and clarify how its entries relate to the sensitivity bounds $\tau$ and $\Phi$.

comments.title: Zero-count singularity in sensitivity bounds
comments.quote: $\tau^{(1)}_{n,m}=\sup_{i\in[k]}\left(\sum_{l=0}^{o}\sum_{s=0}^{o}\frac{l}{N^{(n)}_{i}}\cdot|\alpha_{l,s}|\cdot g_{l}(N^{(n)}_{i},n)g_{s}(N^{(m)}_{i},m)\right)$
comments.explanation: The sensitivity bound $\tau^{(1)}_{n,m}$ contains terms proportional to $1/N^{(n)}_i$. If any bin $i$ has zero observed count in the sample ($N^{(n)}_i = 0$), this term becomes undefined (division by zero), causing $\tau$ and consequently $Z$ to be infinite. This renders the concentration bound trivial or mathematically invalid for typical empirical distributions where zero counts are common. Rewrite the definition of $\tau^{(1)}_{n,m}$ to include a smoothing term or assume $N^{(n)}_i \ge 1$ to prevent division by zero.

comments.title: Remark numbering misaligned with section heading
comments.quote:
iscussed in section 4.1.3, power-MMD is a subset of polynomial GPK. According to Theorem 2, any member $\mathrm{GPK}_{\mathcal{F},\phi_{\rho}}(\bm{p},\bm{q})$ in power-MMD has a default-plugin-estimator with the mapping function $f_{\phi}(S_{i,n_{1}},S_{i,m_{1}})=g_{\rho}(N_{i},n_{1})-g_{\rho}(M_{i},m_{1})$

###### Remark 3.1.

When $\rho=1$,
comments.explanation: The section is explicitly titled '5.1 plugin-estimators of power-MMD', yet the remark contained within it is numbered 'Remark 3.1.' This creates a structural inconsistency that disrupts the document's hierarchy and makes cross-referencing difficult for readers. The remark number should be updated to align with the section numbering (e.g., Remark 5.1) or the section title should be corrected if this content was intended to belong in Chapter 3.

comments.title: Undefined kernel subscript $\phi_l$ breaks parameter continuity
comments.quote: When $\rho=1$, the power-MMD return to the original MMD case. Remarkably, the default-plugin-estimator of this case is equivalent to the linear time statistic $\mathrm{MMD}_{t}^{2}$ proposed in *(Gretton et al., 2012)*: $\mathrm{GPK}_{E}[\mathcal{F},\phi_{l},X,Y]=\mathrm{MMD}_{t}^{2}[\mathcal{F},X,Y]$
comments.explanation: The text explicitly sets $\rho=1$ in the preceding sentence but abruptly introduces the subscript $l$ in $\phi_l$ within the displayed equation. This notation is undefined in the immediate context and breaks consistency with the $\rho$ parameter used throughout the section. Readers will be confused about whether $l$ denotes a different kernel family or is simply a typographical substitution for the value 1. The subscript should be changed to $1$ to maintain notational continuity and clearly indicate that the linear-time MMD corresponds to the polynomial degree $\rho=1$.

comments.title: Equivalence claim between plugin estimator and linear-time statistic lacks qualification
comments.quote: MMD case. Remarkably, the default-plugin-estimator of this case is equivalent to the linear time statistic $\mathrm{MMD}_{t}^{2}$ proposed in *(Gretton et al., 2012)*:
comments.explanation: The plugin estimator typically computes the empirical MMD$^2$ directly from samples, yielding an $O(N^2)$ statistic. The linear-time statistic $mMD_{t}^{2}$ (Gretton et al., 2012) is specifically designed to be $O(N)$ by using a U-statistic decomposition with a specific kernel expansion. Stating they are 'equivalent' without qualification is misleading; they generally differ in variance and computational complexity. The authors should specify the sense of equivalence (e.g., asymptotic distribution, expectation, or under a specific kernel expansion) or correct the claim if they refer to a different estimator. coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a generalized probability kernel (GPK) for discrete distributions, focusing on polynomial GPK and its application to two-sample testing via power-MMD. It aims to connect discrete distribution-property estimation with kernel-based hypothesis testing. The paper provides definitions, theoretical results on unbiased estimators and deviation bounds, and discusses potential applications.

The paper introduces an interesting framework (GPK) that generalizes existing discrepancy measures like MMD and probability product kernels. The connection to Bernstein polynomials for constructing unbiased estimators is a clever theoretical contribution. However, the paper suffers from critical gaps: the main theoretical results (Theorems 2 and 3) are not fully proven in the main text, the deviation bounds appear to contain errors, the practical utility is not demonstrated through any experiments or simulations, and the claims about power-MMD outperforming MMD are unsupported. The paper is incomplete and does not meet the standards of a top venue in its current form.

Overview issues:

- Proofs of main theoretical results are incomplete or missing: Theorem 2 and Theorem 3 are stated in the main text, but their proofs are relegated to the appendix. The proof of Theorem 2 in Appendix A.2 relies on an identity from Qian et al. (2011) but does not verify that the unbiasedness holds for the specific structure of GPK, which involves a product of two f_phi terms and the gram matrix K. The proof jumps from the expectation of a product to the product of expectations without justification—this is only valid if the terms are independent, but N_i^{(n1)} and N_j^{(n2)} are not independent across different i,j. This is a serious gap. The proof of Theorem 3 is similarly sketchy and does not verify the Lipschitz conditions required for McDiarmid's inequality. Without rigorous proofs, the core theoretical claims of the paper are unsubstantiated.

- Deviation bound in Theorem 3 appears to contain errors: The expression for Z in Theorem 3 involves terms like τ^{(1)}_{n1,m1} which are defined as suprema over i of a ratio that includes N_i^{(n1)} in the denominator. When N_i^{(n1)} = 0, this ratio is undefined (division by zero). The paper does not address this. Furthermore, the bound in Corollary 3.2 simplifies to 2e^{-2a^2/(8ρ^2 K_max^2)}, which does not depend on sample size n. This is suspicious—a deviation bound that does not shrink with n cannot be correct for a consistent estimator. The derivation bounding Φ_{n,m} ≤ 2 uses the inequality (∑ a_i)^ρ ≤ ∑ a_i^ρ, which is false for ρ > 1 (the inequality goes the opposite direction by Jensen). This error invalidates the claimed bound and the subsequent test threshold in Corollary 3.3.

- No empirical validation or simulation study: The paper proposes a new two-sample test (power-MMD) and claims it outperforms the linear-time MMD of Gretton et al. (2012), yet it contains no experiments, simulations, or real-data applications. For a methodology paper at a top venue, this is a critical omission. There is no demonstration that the test controls Type I error, has power against alternatives, or that the parameter ρ can be chosen effectively. The paper also does not compare against any existing two-sample tests for discrete data. Without empirical evidence, the practical value of the proposed framework remains entirely speculative.

- Unsupported claim that power-MMD with ρ=1 outperforms linear-time MMD: Section 5.3.1 states that because the deviation bound for power-MMD with ρ=1 is 'tighter' than the bound in Gretton et al. (2012), the test 'will certainly have a better performance.' This is a non-sequitur. A tighter theoretical bound does not guarantee better finite-sample performance—the bound is an upper bound, and the actual variance of the estimator could be larger. Moreover, the paper's bound in Corollary 3.2 does not depend on n, which is unrealistic. The claim of superiority is not justified theoretically or empirically.

- The two-sample test procedure is not properly developed: Corollary 3.3 provides an acceptance region based on the deviation bound, but this is not a valid hypothesis test. The threshold uses the bound Z which depends on the data (through the counts N_i), making it a data-dependent threshold. The paper does not discuss how to obtain a valid p-value or how to calibrate the test to achieve the desired level α. The procedure described is essentially a concentration inequality applied to the test statistic, not a proper test with provable Type I error control. This is a fundamental flaw in the statistical methodology.

- Numerical stability fix in Corollary 3.4 is not properly justified: The paper acknowledges that power-MMD with ρ > 1 may suffer from numerical instability because g_ρ(N_i,n) can be very small. The proposed fix replaces n with S_N (the maximum count) in the denominator, but this changes the estimator. It is not shown that the resulting quantity is an unbiased estimator of anything, nor that it has any relationship to the original GPK. The inequality bounding the test statistic is not derived, and the notation is inconsistent (e.g., using S_N in g_ρ but not defining what g_ρ with S_N means when S_N may not equal the sample size). This section appears to be an incomplete attempt to address a practical problem without proper theoretical grounding.

- Scope and limitations are not adequately discussed: The paper focuses on discrete distributions with finite support of known size k. It does not discuss what happens when k is large relative to sample size, which is a key challenge in discrete distribution estimation. The paper claims GPK can handle cases where values have no meaningful similarity (Section 3.2) but does not develop this point. The discussion of limitations in Section 6 is vague, stating that performance for ρ>1 and non-polynomial GPK is 'left for future work.' A paper proposing a new methodology should provide clearer guidance on when it works and when it fails.

- Missing concrete parametric example for power-MMD: The paper defines power-MMD but never computes it for a fully specified discrete distribution model. A concrete example—e.g., two multinomial distributions with known probability vectors—would help readers calibrate intuitions about how the statistic behaves, how the bias and variance of the plugin estimator scale with ρ, and how the test threshold works. The authors should compute the power-MMD statistic and its deviation bound explicitly for a simple case, such as two Bernoulli distributions or two distributions on three categories with specified probabilities, and show the resulting acceptance region for a given α.

- No guidance on choosing the parameter ρ in practice: The paper introduces ρ as a key parameter of power-MMD but provides no practical guidance on how to select it. Section 5.3.2 acknowledges that performance for ρ>1 is unclear and that numerical stability may be an issue, but it offers no heuristic, data-driven selection method, or sensitivity analysis. For a proposed methodology to be usable, the authors should discuss how ρ affects the bias-variance tradeoff, suggest a default value or a cross-validation scheme, and demonstrate robustness to misspecification in simulations.

- Two-sample test procedure lacks proper calibration: Corollary 3.3 provides an acceptance region based on a concentration inequality, but the threshold depends on the data through Z (which involves the counts N_i). This is not a valid hypothesis test with provable Type I error control—it is essentially a data-dependent bound. The paper does not explain how to obtain a valid p-value or how to calibrate the test to achieve the desired level α. The authors should either derive a valid test using a permutation approach or provide a rigorous analysis showing that the proposed procedure controls Type I error uniformly over the null.

Recommendation: reject

Revision targets:

- Provide complete, rigorous proofs of Theorems 2 and 3, addressing the independence issues and division-by-zero problems.

- Correct the deviation bounds—the current bounds do not depend on sample size and contain mathematical errors (e.g., reversed inequality).

- Include a simulation study demonstrating that the power-MMD test controls Type I error, has power against alternatives, and compares favorably to existing methods (e.g., linear-time MMD, other discrete two-sample tests).

- Develop a proper hypothesis testing framework with valid p-values or calibrated thresholds, rather than relying on a concentration inequality bound.

- Clarify the practical choice of ρ and provide guidance or a data-driven method for selecting it.

comments.title: Product GPK coefficient index mismatch
comments.quote: The product GPK is a subset of polynomial GPK where $\alpha_{l,0}=1$, and all other coefficients are $0$. the corresponding mapping function is: $\phi(\bm{p},\bm{q})=\bm{p}^{l}$
comments.explanation: In Definition 4 (polynomial GPK), the kernel is $GPK_{F,\phi}(p,q) = \sum_{i=0}^d \sum_{j=0}^d \alpha_{i,j} (p^i)^T K (q^j)$ with mapping $\phi(p,q) = \sum_{i=0}^d \sum_{j=0}^d \alpha_{i,j} p^i q^j$. Setting $\alpha_{l,0}=1$ gives $\phi(p,q) = p^l q^0 = p^l$, but the kernel becomes $(p^l)^T K (q^0) = (p^l)^T K \mathbf{1}$, which is not the product kernel $(p^l)^T K (q^l)$ unless $l=0$. The product GPK should correspond to $\alpha_{l,l}=1$ to obtain $\phi(p,q)=p^l q^l$ and kernel $(p^l)^T K (q^l)$. Rewrite the quoted text as: 'The product GPK is a subset of polynomial GPK where $\alpha_{l,l}=1$, and all other coefficients are $0$. The corresponding mapping function is: $\phi(\bm{p},\bm{q})=\bm{p}^l \bm{q}^l$.'
